beneater.be_a.board.content:
The A Register Board

Chips: 2× 74x173, 74x245.

  • Two 74x173 4-bit registers hold the byte; their D inputs tap the bus, their shared input-enable is AI, and their shared CLR ties to the reset line.
  • Their Q outputs run two places at once: off-board to the ALU (always), and into the 74x245 buffer whose OE is AO (onto the bus, only when asked).
  • This exact pattern — latch on "-in", buffer out on "-out" — is the template for every register in the machine. Once you can read this board, you can read them all.
This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_a.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_a.description: The machine’s working value. Latches from the bus on AI, always feeds the ALU, and can drive the bus on AO. Nearly every result lands here.
beneater.be_a.title: 9. A Register: the Accumulator
beneater.be_a.why.content:
A Register — the Accumulator

The A register is where the machine works. Load a number — it goes to A. Add — the result goes back to A. Print — A is what gets displayed. Nearly every instruction either reads it, writes it, or both, which is why this style of register earned the name accumulator: results accumulate here.

Three connections define it:

  • AI — latch a byte from the bus (that's LDA, LDI, and the write-back of ADD/SUB).
  • Its value is permanently wired to the ALU as the "A" operand — no control signal needed; the ALU always sees it.
  • AO — drive its value onto the bus (used by STA and OUT).
beneater.be_a.why.title: What it does
beneater.be_alu.board.content:
The ALU Board

Chips: 2× 74x283, 2× 74x86, 74x245, 74x32.

  • The two 74x283 4-bit adders chain their carry (spot the yellow wire from the low chip's carry-out to the high chip's carry-in) to add full bytes.
  • The two 74x86 XOR banks sit on the B inputs — the conditional bit-flipper for subtraction. SU feeds both the XORs and the low adder's carry-in.
  • The 74x32 OR tree checks for an all-zero result (the zero flag); the top adder's carry-out is the carry flag.
  • The 74x245 puts the sum on the bus when EO is active. Yellow LEDs below show the live sum.
This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_alu.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_alu.description: Two 74x283 adders compute A+B; XOR gates and a carry-in turn the same adder into a subtractor via two’s complement. Produces the carry and zero flags.
beneater.be_alu.quiz.content:
Quick Check

How does the SAP-1 subtract, given that it only contains adder chips?
beneater.be_alu.quiz.title: Quick check
beneater.be_alu.title: 10. ALU: the Math
beneater.be_alu.why.content:
ALU — the Math

The arithmetic logic unit continuously computes A + B — it has no clock and no memory. It just sits between the two registers, and its sum is always live. EO gates that result onto the bus when an instruction wants it.

The elegant part is subtraction. When the SU signal is set, XOR gates flip every bit of B, and the adder's carry-in adds 1:

A + (B XOR 11111111) + 1  =  A + (−B)

That's two's complement: flip the bits and add one to negate. The same adder circuit does both operations — no separate subtractor exists.

The ALU also reports two facts about its result: did the add carry past 8 bits (CF), and is the result exactly zero (ZF)? Those two bits, latched by the flags register, are what make conditional jumps — decisions — possible.
beneater.be_alu.why.title: What it does
beneater.be_b.board.content:
The B Register Board

Chips: 2× 74x173.

Compare this board with the A register's: the same two 74x173 latches, the same bus taps on the D inputs, the same shared BI input-enable and reset wiring — but no 74x245. Where A had a buffer gating its value onto the bus, B's outputs head only one place: the ALU.

Red LEDs below show its contents in the running machine.

This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_b.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_b.description: Latches the ALU’s second operand from the bus on BI and feeds it straight to the adder. Unlike A, it has no path back onto the bus.
beneater.be_b.title: 11. B Register: the Second Operand
beneater.be_b.why.content:
B Register — the Second Operand

Adding takes two numbers. A holds the first; B holds the second. During ADD 14, the machine fetches mem[14] from RAM into B (RO + BI), and only then fires the ALU's result back into A.

B is deliberately a lesser register than A:

  • It latches from the bus on BI — same as any register.
  • Its outputs are permanently wired to the ALU's "B" input.
  • But it has no output buffer — the classic SAP-1 B register cannot drive the bus at all. It exists solely to feed the adder.

That asymmetry is a design lesson in itself: every part costs chips, and the minimal machine gives each register only the connections its instruction set actually uses.
beneater.be_b.why.title: What it does
beneater.be_clock.board.content:
The Clock Board

Chips: 555, 74x08, 74x04.

On this board the 555, AND, and inverter are placed for fidelity to Ben's build, and a built-in 1 Hz clock source (the small square component to their right) drives the CLK net directly — in the full machine that net fans out to every register and counter.

Things to spot:

  • Every chip's VCC and GND wires run to the power rails — red to +5 V, dark to ground. Every module in the machine repeats this pattern.
  • The clock source ticks on its own — it's the one component here that moves without any input.
This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_clock.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_clock.description: A 555-based clock with a halt gate. Nothing in the CPU moves except on its edge — single-step it and the whole machine walks one micro-step at a time.
beneater.be_clock.title: 2. Clock: the Heartbeat
beneater.be_clock.why.content:
Clock — the Heartbeat

Every register, the program counter, and the control step counter all share one CLK line. Nothing changes except on its edge, which is what keeps the whole machine synchronized.

In Ben Eater's build, a 555 timer wired astable makes the square wave. A 74x08 AND gate plus a 74x04 inverter sit between the 555 and the rest of the machine so the HLT (halt) control signal can freeze the clock — that's how the HLT instruction stops the computer. A second 555 debounces a manual single-step button.

Slowing the clock down (or stopping it entirely between manual steps) is the SAP-1's superpower as a teaching machine: at 1 Hz you can watch every transfer.
beneater.be_clock.why.title: Why a clock
beneater.be_control.board.content:
The Control Board

Chips: 74x161, 74x138, 2× 28C16 EEPROM, 74x04.

  • The 74x161 is the step counter; the 74x138 decodes the step number (Ben uses it to reset the sequence).
  • The two 28C16 EEPROMs are the stars. Their address pins are fed by the step counter (A0–A2), the IR's opcode (A3–A6), and the two flag bits (A7–A8). Their sixteen combined output pins are the machine's sixteen control signals — HLT MI RI RO IO II AI AO EO SU BI OI CE CO J FI.
  • Both ROMs are pre-loaded with the SAP-1 microcode — the same fetch steps and per-opcode sequences you'll walk through in the final lesson.
This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_control.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_control.description: A step counter, a decoder, and two microcode EEPROMs. Addressed by {opcode, step, flags}, they fire the 16 control signals that run every other module.
beneater.be_control.quiz.content:
Quick Check

You want to add a brand-new instruction to the SAP-1 (say, a "decrement A" opcode). What has to change?
beneater.be_control.quiz.title: Quick check
beneater.be_control.title: 8. Control Logic: the Conductor
beneater.be_control.why.content:
Control Logic — the Conductor

Every module so far responds to control signals — CO, MI, RO, AI, … But who asserts them, and in what order? This module. It is the brain of the machine.

The mechanism is beautifully simple:

  • A step counter walks through micro-steps T0, T1, T2, … one per clock tick.
  • Two EEPROMs hold the microcode: a lookup table addressed by { opcode, step, carry-flag, zero-flag }. Whatever 16-bit word lives at that address is the set of control lines to assert on this tick.

That's it — the "brain" is a table lookup. The opcode picks a row, the step counter walks the columns, and the outputs pull the strings of every other module.

The deep consequence: change the ROM contents and you change the instruction set. The hardware doesn't move; the machine just means something different. That boundary — hardware below, stored microcode above — is where "software" begins.
beneater.be_control.why.title: What it does
beneater.be_flags.board.content:
The Flags Board

Chips: 74x173.

One 4-bit register, using only two of its bits: D1 latches the carry flag, D2 the zero flag (the unused inputs are tied low). FI is its input-enable; its two Q outputs run to the control ROMs' A8 and A7 address pins.

Yellow LEDs below show the latched flags.

Honest caveat: in this build the zero-flag detector isn't wired into the latch yet, so JZ won't actually branch — the carry path (JC) is fully functional. The architecture story above is exactly right; one wire is still on the to-do list.
This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_flags.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_flags.description: One 74x173 latches the carry and zero bits on FI. The control ROMs read them as address lines — that’s the whole mechanism behind conditional jumps.
beneater.be_flags.title: 12. Flags: How a CPU Decides
beneater.be_flags.why.content:
Flags Register — Remembering the Result's Status

Everything so far moves and combines data. But a computer isn't just a calculator because it can decide — run different code depending on a result. The entire mechanism of decision fits in one tiny register.

When FI is asserted (during ADD/SUB), it latches two bits from the ALU:

  • CF — the operation carried past 8 bits.
  • ZF — the result was exactly zero.

Remember that the control ROMs are addressed by { opcode, step, CF, ZF }. A JC (jump-if-carry) opcode points at microcode that loads the PC only in the CF=1 half of the table; in the CF=0 half, the same steps do nothing. The flag literally selects which microcode runs.

That's an if statement, in hardware. Loops that terminate, branches that react to data — all of it rides on these two latched bits.
beneater.be_flags.why.title: What it does
beneater.be_ir.board.content:
The IR Board

Chips: 2× 74x173, 74x245.

  • The two 74x173 4-bit registers latch the byte together — shared CLK, shared II input-enable (follow the purple wire linking their IE pins).
  • The high register's Q outputs leave the board for the control logic — that's the opcode path. Nothing gates them; the control logic always sees the current opcode.
  • The low register's Q outputs feed the 74x245, whose OE is wired to IO — the operand only touches the bus when asked. Its unused high inputs are tied low, which is why an operand on the bus reads as 0–15.
  • Red LEDs below show the full latched instruction byte.
This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_ir.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_ir.description: Two 74x173s latch the instruction byte on II. Top nibble = opcode, straight to the control logic; bottom nibble = operand, back onto the bus on IO.
beneater.be_ir.title: 7. Instruction Register: the Current Instruction
beneater.be_ir.why.content:
Instruction Register — Holding the Instruction Apart

During fetch, the instruction byte arrives from RAM over the bus. But the bus is about to be reused — the very next micro-step needs it for data. So the instruction gets parked in its own register: when II is asserted, the IR latches the whole byte.

Then the byte is split in half, and the two halves lead very different lives:

  • Top nibble = opcode. Wired straight to the control logic; it decides what this instruction does.
  • Bottom nibble = operand — an address or a small constant. On IO, a buffer puts it back onto the bus, e.g. to supply LDA's address to the MAR or LDI's value to the A register.

So 0x1F means "opcode 1, operand 15" — LDA 15. The format is the machine's entire instruction encoding.
beneater.be_ir.why.title: What it does
beneater.be_mar.board.content:
The MAR Board

Chips: 74x173, plus a 4-way DIP switch.

  • The 74x173 is a 4-bit D register: its D inputs tap the bus's low 4 bits, its IE (input-enable) pin is wired to MI, and its Q outputs run to the RAM module's address mux.
  • The DIP switches are the manual side: on Ben's real machine you flip these to dial an address by hand when loading a program. (In this build the pre-loaded demo program makes them optional.)
  • The amber LEDs below show which address is currently selected.
This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_mar.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_mar.description: A single 74x173 latches 4 bits of the bus on MI and holds them as RAM’s address lines. DIP switches let you dial an address by hand.
beneater.be_mar.title: 5. Memory Address Register: Pointing at a Word
beneater.be_mar.why.content:
Memory Address Register — Point RAM at a Word

RAM holds 16 bytes, but it can only show you one at a time: whichever one its 4 address lines select. Those address lines need to hold steady while RAM is being read or written — you can't have the address wobbling mid-access.

So the machine gives the address its own register. When MI (memory-address-in) is asserted, the MAR latches the low 4 bits of the bus on the clock edge, and from then on it holds RAM pointed at that word — no matter what the bus does next.

Every memory access in every instruction starts the same way: put an address on the bus, assert MI, tick. During fetch that address comes from the PC; during LDA/ADD/STA it comes from the instruction's operand.
beneater.be_mar.why.title: What it does
beneater.be_output.board.content:
The Output Board

Chips: 74x377, 28C16, 74x107, 74x139, 4× 7-segment.

  • The 74x377 is an 8-bit latch — the register that grabs the bus on OI.
  • Its byte feeds the 28C16 decode ROM's address pins; the ROM's outputs are segment patterns.
  • The 74x107 flip-flops and 74x139 decoder are Ben's digit-scanning circuit: they cycle through the four digits fast enough that all appear lit. (In this build the scan is placeholder-wired; the ROM and displays are real.)
This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_output.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_output.description: A 74x377 latches a byte on OI; an EEPROM converts it to 7-segment patterns and four displays show it in decimal. The machine’s only way to print.
beneater.be_output.title: 13. Output: the Display
beneater.be_output.why.content:
Output — the Display

A CPU that can't show you anything might as well not exist. The output module is the machine's one window to the world: when OI is asserted (that's the OUT instruction), it latches whatever is on the bus and shows it as a decimal number on 7-segment displays.

Decimal is the clever part. The latched byte is binary; a display digit needs a specific 7-segment pattern. Rather than build binary-to-decimal conversion from gates (a lot of gates), Ben uses another EEPROM as a lookup table: address = the byte plus which digit we're rendering; data = the segment pattern for that digit. Pure precomputed answers.

Note the trick echoes the control logic: when logic gets complicated, bake it into a ROM. Same idea, second appearance.
beneater.be_output.why.title: What it does
beneater.be_overview.description: What the SAP-1 is, and the two ideas that tie a dozen breadboards into one computer: a shared 8-bit bus with a single-driver rule, and a clock that keeps every module in lock-step.
beneater.be_overview.quiz_bus.content:
Quick Check

At one instant during a program, how many modules may be driving the bus, and how many may be listening?
beneater.be_overview.quiz_bus.title: Quick check
beneater.be_overview.the_bus.content:
The Bus — a Shared 8-bit Highway

Every module connects to one common set of 8 wires: the bus. It is the only road data travels between modules. The single most important rule of the whole machine:

Only one module may drive the bus at any instant — but any number may listen and latch a copy of it.

If two modules drove the bus at once you'd get a short and garbage data. That's why every module that can put a value on the bus does it through a 74x245 tri-state buffer: its outputs disconnect (go Hi-Z) unless the module's output-enable signal is active — CO (counter out), RO (RAM out), IO (instruction operand out), AO (A out), or EO (ALU out).

Modules that need to capture the bus assert an "…-in" signal instead: MI, RI, II, AI, BI, OI, FI, or J.

This is the tri-state idea from the intro course doing its signature job: letting many chips share one set of wires without contention.
beneater.be_overview.the_bus.title: The bus
beneater.be_overview.the_clock.content:
The Clock — Everything in Lock-step

The second unifying idea: no register changes except on a clock edge. On each tick, the modules whose "-in" signal is active grab whatever is on the bus right now, and the counters advance.

That single shared tick is what keeps a dozen separate boards acting as one machine instead of twelve loose chips. Between edges, the control logic sets up who drives and who listens; the edge itself is the moment the data actually moves.

Set the clock to single-step (as you can in the full simulator) and the whole computer walks one micro-step at a time — you can literally watch each transfer happen.

Keep these two ideas in mind through every module lesson: each module is just something that drives the bus, listens to the bus, or both — on the beat of the clock.
beneater.be_overview.the_clock.title: The clock
beneater.be_overview.title: 1. The Big Picture: One Bus, One Clock
beneater.be_overview.welcome.content:
A Computer You Can Point At

This course walks through a working recreation of Ben Eater's SAP-1 ("Simple As Possible") 8-bit computer, built entirely from 74-series logic chips inside 74Sim. It runs real programs — the demo program counts 1, 2, 3, … on a 7-segment display.

Just like Ben's video series, every module lives on its own breadboard: clock, program counter, memory address register, RAM, instruction register, control logic, two data registers, an ALU, a flags register, and an output display. Each of the next twelve lessons puts one of those real boards next to the text, so you can see exactly which chips do the job and how they're wired.

The point is not to be fast or clever. It's to let you see, one clock edge at a time, how a pile of logic chips becomes a computer.

No prior CPU knowledge needed, but the "Introduction to Digital Hardware" course (especially the tri-state and counters lessons) is a good warm-up.
beneater.be_overview.welcome.title: A computer you can point at
beneater.be_pc.board.content:
The PC Board

Chips: 74x161 counter, 74x245 buffer.

  • The 74x161 is a 4-bit binary counter: CLK ticks it, ENP is the CE count-enable, LOAD is the J jump input, and its A–D load pins tap the bus's low 4 bits.
  • The 74x245 tri-state buffer sits between the counter's outputs and the bus. Its OE pin is driven by CO — the counter only appears on the bus when the control logic says so.
  • The green LEDs on the lower board show the current address — in the running machine you watch them count 0, 1, 2, … then snap backwards on a jump.
This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_pc.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_pc.description: A 74x161 counter holds the address of the next instruction; a 74x245 puts it on the bus on CO. Count-enable steps the program in order; the jump-load input is how JMP works.
beneater.be_pc.quiz.content:
Quick Check

How does the SAP-1 execute a JMP 6 instruction?
beneater.be_pc.quiz.title: Quick check
beneater.be_pc.title: 4. Program Counter: What Runs Next
beneater.be_pc.why.content:
Program Counter — What's Next

A program is a list of instructions sitting in memory at addresses 0, 1, 2, … Something has to remember where we are in that list. That is the entire job of the program counter (PC).

It holds an address from 0–15 and responds to three control signals:

  • CE (count-enable) — bump the address +1. Asserted once during every fetch, so the program runs in order.
  • CO (counter-out) — drive the current address onto the bus, so the memory address register can grab it.
  • J (jump) — load a new address from the bus instead of counting. This is how JMP (and the conditional jumps) work: overwrite "where we are" and the program continues from somewhere else.

Counting in order is what makes it a machine that runs programs; loading from the bus is what makes those programs able to loop and branch.
beneater.be_pc.why.title: What it does
beneater.be_ram.board.content:
The RAM Board

Chips: 2× 74x189, 74x157, 74x00, 74x245, plus DIP switches.

  • Each 74x189 is a 16×4 static RAM; two side-by-side store 16 bytes (one chip holds the low nibbles, one the high).
  • The 74x157 mux picks the address source: the MAR (when the program runs) or the DIP switches (for hand-programming).
  • Here's the gotcha Ben spends a whole video on: the 74x189's outputs are inverted. The 74x245 buffer both re-inverts the byte and gates it onto the bus when RO is active.
  • The 74x00 gates the write-enable pulse for RI (tied off in this build — RAM is pre-loaded and read-mostly).
This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_ram.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_ram.description: Two 74x189 chips hold the program AND its data — 16 bytes of memory behind an address mux, with a re-inverting 74x245 gating reads onto the bus.
beneater.be_ram.quiz.content:
Quick Check

The SAP-1 stores its program and its data in the same 16 bytes of RAM. What is the deep consequence of that?
beneater.be_ram.quiz.title: Quick check
beneater.be_ram.title: 6. RAM: 16 Bytes of Everything
beneater.be_ram.why.content:
RAM — Where the Program and Data Live

Sixteen bytes. That's the SAP-1's entire memory — and remarkably, it holds both the program and the data in the same address space. That's the von Neumann idea in its smallest possible form: instructions are just bytes in memory, no different from the numbers they operate on.

Two control signals:

  • RO (RAM-out) — drive the byte at the MAR's address onto the bus. Used to fetch instructions and to read data.
  • RI (RAM-in) — write the bus into the byte at the MAR's address. That's STA.

The demo program the machine ships with lives at addresses 0–6, and uses addresses 14 and 15 as its two variables. Program and data, side by side, in the same sixteen bytes.
beneater.be_ram.why.title: What it does
beneater.be_reset.board.content:
The Reset Board

Parts: one push button.

That's the whole module: a push button between the RST net and a power rail. In the full machine, one wire chains from here to the CLR pin of every 74x173 register, the 74x161 program counter, and the 74x161 step counter.

One button, wired to a dozen chips, is what makes the difference between a machine you have to power-cycle and one you can restart with a tap.

This board is the real module lifted straight out of the full CPU — same chips, same wiring. In the complete machine its inputs arrive on the bus and control rails, so in isolation it holds still: this is an anatomy view. Hover over pins and wires to trace the nets.
beneater.be_reset.board.title: On the board
beneater.be_reset.description: One push button pulls the RST line and every register and counter clears to zero. Execution restarts from address 0.
beneater.be_reset.title: 3. Reset: Clear the Machine
beneater.be_reset.why.content:
Reset — Clear the Machine

A computer full of registers has a problem at power-on: every register wakes up holding garbage. The reset line fixes that — and lets you re-run a program from the top at any time.

Pressing the button asserts RST, which is wired to the CLR (clear) input of every register, the program counter, and the control logic's step counter. All of them snap to zero: the PC points at address 0, the step counter starts at T0, and execution restarts from the first instruction.

It's the simplest module in the machine — and a good reminder that a "module" doesn't have to be complicated to matter.
beneater.be_reset.why.title: Why reset
beneater.be_synthesis.cycle.content:
The Instruction Cycle: Fetch, Decode, Execute

You've now met every module. Here is the choreography that makes them a computer. Every instruction begins with the same two fetch steps:

StepSignalsWhat moves
T0CO + MIProgram Counter → bus → Memory Address Register (point RAM at the next instruction)
T1RO + II + CERAM → bus → Instruction Register; counter +1

After the fetch, the opcode now sitting in the IR — plus the step number and the flags — addresses the control ROMs, which fire the right signals for steps T2, T3, … That's the decode and execute. When the instruction is done, the step counter resets and the next fetch begins.

For example, ADD 14 executes as:

StepSignalsWhat moves
T2IO + MIoperand → bus → MAR (address of the number to add)
T3RO + BIRAM → bus → B register (fetch the number)
T4EO + AI + FIALU(A+B) → bus → A; latch the flags
beneater.be_synthesis.cycle.title: The instruction cycle
beneater.be_synthesis.description: Fetch, decode, execute: walk the full instruction cycle through the machine, read the instruction set, trace the demo program — and see why a modern CPU is this same dance at billions of steps per second.
beneater.be_synthesis.generalize.content:
This Is Every CPU Ever Built

Here's the payoff. The machine you just studied is not a toy version of a computer — it is the actual shape of every computer:

  • A program counter remembers where execution is.
  • Instructions are fetched from memory, decoded, executed.
  • An ALU computes; flags record result status; conditional jumps read them to make decisions.
  • Control logic sequences who talks to whom over shared datapaths.

A modern Ryzen or Apple Silicon core does this same dance — fetch, decode, execute — billions of times per second, with wider buses, more registers, caches in front of memory, and dozens of instructions in flight at once. Those are magnificent optimizations, but they are optimizations of this loop. Strip any CPU to its skeleton and you find the SAP-1.

You now know what a computer is: registers on a bus, an adder, a table of microcode, and a clock. Everything else is scale.
beneater.be_synthesis.generalize.title: Every CPU ever built
beneater.be_synthesis.isa.content:
The Instruction Set

Eleven instructions. The opcode is the byte's top nibble; the operand (where used) is the bottom nibble.

OpcodeMnemonicMeaning
0x0NOPdo nothing
0x1LDA addrload A from RAM[addr]
0x2ADD addrA = A + RAM[addr] (sets flags)
0x3SUB addrA = A − RAM[addr] (sets flags)
0x4STA addrstore A into RAM[addr]
0x5LDI valload the 4-bit immediate into A
0x6JMP addrjump to addr
0x7JC addrjump if carry flag set
0x8JZ addrjump if zero flag set
0xEOUTcopy A to the output display
0xFHLThalt the clock

Every one of these is nothing more than a short list of control-signal words in the microcode ROMs, appended to the shared fetch. There is no deeper magic to find — this table is the machine's vocabulary.
beneater.be_synthesis.isa.title: The instruction set
beneater.be_synthesis.program.content:
The Demo Program

RAM ships pre-loaded with a counter that prints 1, 2, 3, … on the display:

AddrInstructionEffect
0LDI 1A = 1
1STA 14mem[14] = 1 (the increment)
2LDA 15A = mem[15] (running total, starts at 0)
3ADD 14A = A + 1
4OUTdisplay A
5STA 15mem[15] = A
6JMP 2loop back to address 2

Watch what's really happening: the program keeps its variables (the increment at address 14, the running total at 15) in the same RAM as its code, uses the ALU through A and B, prints through the output register, and loops forever through the PC's jump input. Every module you've studied earns its keep within seven bytes.
beneater.be_synthesis.program.title: The demo program
beneater.be_synthesis.quiz_fetch.content:
Final Check 1 of 2

Why do the first two micro-steps (T0, T1) of every instruction look exactly the same?
beneater.be_synthesis.quiz_fetch.title: Final check 1
beneater.be_synthesis.quiz_micro.content:
Final Check 2 of 2

During execution, what exactly do the control ROMs look up on each clock tick?

Answer correctly to finish the course — then go single-step the real thing.
beneater.be_synthesis.quiz_micro.title: Final check 2
beneater.be_synthesis.title: 14. Putting It All Together
beneater.be_synthesis.trace.content:
Tracing One Instruction, Ben's Way

In his control-logic video, Ben does something worth copying: he writes a program, then says out loud what the control logic must do on every single clock tick to execute it. Let's do that for the demo program's LDA 15 (it lives at address 2, and address 15 holds the running total).

TickSignalsSay it out loud
T0CO + MI"The program counter says 2 — move that value into the memory address register."
T1RO + II + CE"Take the contents of RAM at address 2 — the byte 0x1F — and put it in the instruction register. Count the PC up to 3."
T2IO + MI"Take the operand nibble of the instruction register — 15 — and move that value into the memory address register."
T3RO + AI"Take the contents of RAM at address 15 and move it into the A register."

Read the right-hand column again and notice: each sentence is just one module driving the bus and one module latching from it. That's all a CPU ever does.

And the microcode ROM? It is exactly that speech, frozen into a table. Every row you could say out loud for every instruction, stored at the address { opcode, step }, replayed by the step counter forever.
beneater.be_synthesis.trace.title: Tracing one instruction
beneater.be_synthesis.whole_machine.cont:
The Whole Machine

Here it is — all twelve boards, wired together. The left column of boards is the CPU, top-to-bottom in the order data flows; the right column is each module's LED readout. The long colored wires running board-to-board are the 8-bit bus; the gray ones are the control signals you now know by name.

The lesson canvas is small for a machine this size, so when you're ready:

Open the full CPU in the simulator

There you can zoom around each board, set the clock to single-step, press reset, and watch the demo program count on the displays — one micro-step per tick, every transfer visible on the LEDs.

Honest caveats if you go probing: the zero-flag detector, the RAM write-button path, and the display's digit-scanning are placeholder-wired in this build — the architecture is faithful, but those three corners are the "next things to wire up."
beneater.blurb: A module-by-module tour of Ben Eater’s SAP-1 breadboard computer, rebuilt from real 74-series chips inside 74Sim. One lesson per module — clock, bus, registers, ALU, RAM, control logic — then a final lesson on how they combine into a working CPU, and why every CPU ever built does the same dance.

intro.and_gate_basics.description: Wire up a 74x08 AND gate to make an LED light when both buttons are pressed.
intro.and_gate_basics.intro.content:
Logic Gates

In this lesson, you'll wire up a 74x08 AND gate to make an LED light up only when both push buttons are pressed.

The breadboard already has:

  • Two push buttons (with pull down resistors)
  • One red LED (with a current limiting resistor)

Your job is to place an AND gate chip and wire everything together.

Tip: The push buttons connect to VCC when pressed. The pull down resistors ensure the signal is LOW (0V) when the button is not pressed.

intro.and_gate_basics.intro.hint.1.label: Button B

intro.and_gate_basics.place_chip.content:
Step 1: Place the Chip

Click the Place 74x08 button in the toolbar above, then click on the breadboard to place the chip.

The chip needs to straddle the center channel place it so it spans rows E and F (the gap in the middle).

Tip: Click on the breadboard between columns 18-24 to place the chip. The first click sets pin 1, the second click confirms placement across the channel.
Place the chip here, across the channel
intro.and_gate_basics.place_chip.title: Place the 74x08 AND Gate
intro.and_gate_basics.test.content:
Step 7: Test It!

Your circuit is wired! Now test it:

  1. Click and hold both push buttons at the same time
  2. The LED should light up!
  3. Release one button the LED should turn off

This is the AND gate in action: the output is HIGH only when both inputs are HIGH.

Tip: Click and hold one button, then while holding, click the other button too. The LED will light when both are pressed.

intro.and_gate_basics.test.hint.1.label: …and button B together

intro.and_gate_basics.wire_gnd.content:
Step 3: Wire GND (Ground)

Now connect pin 7 (GND) to the ground rail (the - row at the bottom).

Pin 7 is the bottom-right pin of the chip.

Tip: Click "Wire", then click pin 7 of the chip, then click the - rail below it.
intro.and_gate_basics.wire_gnd.title: Wire GND to the Chip
intro.and_gate_basics.wire_input_a.conte:
Step 4: Wire Button A → Input 1A

Connect Push Button A's signal (column 8, any row on the same strip) to the AND gate's pin 1 (1A).

Pin 1 is the bottom-left pin of the chip the first pin on the lower row.

Tip: The button's signal is available on any hole in column 8, rows 0-4 (same strip). Wire from there to pin 1 of the AND gate.
intro.and_gate_basics.wire_input_a.title: Wire Button A to AND Input
intro.and_gate_basics.wire_input_b.conte:
Step 5: Wire Button B → Input 1B

Connect Push Button B's signal (column 12) to the AND gate's pin 2 (1B).

Pin 2 is the second pin from the left on the bottom row.
intro.and_gate_basics.wire_input_b.title: Wire Button B to AND Input
intro.and_gate_basics.wire_output.conten:
Step 6: Wire Output → LED

Connect the AND gate's pin 3 (1Y, the output) to the LED's anode (column 30, row 5 or above same strip as the LED's top pin).

Pin 3 is the third pin from the left on the bottom row of the chip.

Tip: The LED's anode is at column 30. Wire from pin 3 of the AND gate to any hole on column 30, rows 5-8 (same strip as the LED's top end).
intro.and_gate_basics.wire_output.title: Wire AND Output to LED
intro.and_gate_basics.wire_vcc.content:
Step 2: Wire VCC (Power)

The 74x08 needs power to operate. Connect pin 14 (VCC) to the positive power rail (the + row at the top).

Pin 14 is the top-left pin of the chip.

Tip: Click "Wire" in the toolbar, then click pin 14, then click the + rail directly above it.

intro.blurb: New to digital logic or the 74 series? This introduction walks you through the fundamentals step by step. No prior experience required. Each lesson builds on the last, from a single logic gate all the way to flip flops and counters.
intro.debug_circuit.description: Find and fix two errors in a combinational logic circuit.
Step 3: Add the Missing Pull Down Resistor

The push button still doesn't work correctly. When it's not pressed, its input line is floating not connected to any defined voltage.

Every button needs a pull down resistor to GND so it reads LOW when not pressed.

The other switch inputs have pull down resistors, but the push button is missing one.

To fix:

  1. Click Resistor in the toolbar
  2. Place a resistor from the button's signal column (column 9, on the lower breadboard tile) to the GND rail
Tip: Place the resistor from hole 0:1:main:9:4 down to the GND rail at 0:1:power:9:2. Use a 1k or 10k ohm value.
intro.debug_circuit.find_wire_error.cont:
Step 2: Fix the Wrong Wire

Trace the signal path from the NOT gate to the AND gate:

  1. The NOT gate (74x04) inverts Switch 2 at gate 3 (pin 5 → pin 6, which is 3Y)
  2. The inverted output should connect to the AND gate's pin 5 (2B)
  3. But look at the blue wire it goes to the wrong row!

To fix:

  1. Click the wrong wire's endpoint to select it
  2. Press Delete to remove it
  3. Click Wire and draw a new wire from the NOT output (col 17, row F) to AND pin 2B (col 8, row F)
Tip: The wire needs to connect column 17 row 6 (bottom side) to column 8 row 6 (bottom side) both on the F row.
intro.debug_circuit.intro.content:
Debug a Combinational Circuit

This circuit uses three 74 series chips to implement a logic function:

  • 74x08 AND gate
  • 74x04 NOT gate (inverter)
  • 74x32 OR gate

The circuit should light the LED when: Switch 1 is ON AND Switch 2 is OFF (inverted), then OR'd with the push button signal.

But it has two errors. Your job is to find and fix them both.
intro.debug_circuit.intro.hint.0.label: 74x08 AND
intro.debug_circuit.intro.hint.1.label: 74x04 NOT
intro.debug_circuit.intro.hint.2.label: 74x32 OR
intro.debug_circuit.intro.hint.3.label: LED
intro.debug_circuit.intro.title: The Circuit Has Bugs
intro.debug_circuit.observe.content:
Step 1: Observe

Try toggling the switches and pressing the push button. Notice the circuit doesn't behave correctly.

The expected behavior:

  • Turn Switch 1 ON and Switch 2 OFF
  • The NOT gate inverts Switch 2 → HIGH
  • AND gate: Switch 1 (HIGH) AND NOT(Switch 2) (HIGH) → output HIGH
  • OR gate passes this to the LED → LED ON

But when you try this, the LED doesn't light correctly. Something is wrong with the wiring.

Tip: Click "Next" when you've observed the problem and are ready to start debugging.
intro.debug_circuit.observe.hint.0.label: Switch 1
intro.debug_circuit.observe.hint.1.label: Switch 2

intro.debug_circuit.observe.title: Observe the Problem
intro.debug_circuit.title: Lesson 3: Debug a Circuit
intro.debug_circuit.verify.content:
Step 4: Verify Your Fix

Now test the circuit to confirm it works:

  1. Turn Switch 1 ON (click it)
  2. Keep Switch 2 OFF
  3. The LED should light up!

You can also try pressing the push button the OR gate means the LED should also light when the button is pressed (regardless of switch positions).

Tip: Toggle the switches by clicking them. Click and hold the push button.

intro.encoders_decoders.active_low.conte:
Two Habits of Real Chips

The 74x148 will introduce you to two conventions you'll meet on almost every datasheet from now on.

1. Its inputs are active-low. "Select me" is signalled with 0, not 1. Each input line idles HIGH through a pull-up resistor; closing that line's switch connects it to GND:

Why design it "upside down"? Historical electronics reasons (TTL chips could pull LOW much harder than HIGH), but the convention stuck and half the control pins in the catalog use it. Watch for the bar over the name or a small circle on the symbol.

2. It's a priority encoder. What if two keys are pressed at once — which number wins? The 74x148 answers: the highest one. Inputs 3 and 5 both active → output says 5, cleanly, every time. Without a priority rule the output would be garbage whenever inputs overlap.
intro.encoders_decoders.active_low.title: Priority and Active-Low
intro.encoders_decoders.board_838.conten:
Try It: 8 → 3 → 8

The full round trip on one board:

  • Eight switches (left) are the inputs — remember, active-low: flipping a switch ON connects its line to GND, selecting it. The 1 k pull-ups above hold unselected lines HIGH.
  • The 74x148 encodes the highest selected line into a 3-bit code, shown on the three red LEDs (middle).
  • The 74x237 decodes those same 3 bits and lights exactly one of the eight yellow LEDs (right).

Things to try:

  1. Flip switch 5 ON — the red LEDs show 101 and yellow LED 5 lights.
  2. Now also flip switch 2 ON. Nothing changes: 5 has priority. Turn 5 off and the display drops to 2.
Complete this step by flipping any switch ON.
intro.encoders_decoders.board_838.title: The 8-3-8 Board
intro.encoders_decoders.description: Squeeze many lines down to a few (encode) and fan them back out (decode): 8 switches → 3 bits → 1 of 8 LEDs.
intro.encoders_decoders.quiz_log2.conten:
Quiz 1 of 2

You need to report which one of 16 lines is active. How few wires can an encoder squeeze that into?
intro.encoders_decoders.quiz_log2.title: Quiz 1 of 2
intro.encoders_decoders.quiz_priority.co:
Quiz 2 of 2

On the board, switches 3 and 6 are both ON (both lines pulled LOW). What does the 74x148 output?
intro.encoders_decoders.title: Lesson 8: Encoders / Decoders
intro.encoders_decoders.why_encode.conte:
Many Lines, Few Wires

Suppose a keypad has 8 keys and you need to tell a distant chip which one is pressed. The obvious way is 8 wires — one per key. But only one key is pressed at a time, so 8 wires to carry one fact is wasteful.

Number the keys 0–7 and send the number in binary instead: 3 wires cover all 8 possibilities, because 2³ = 8. In general, n lines compress into ⌈log₂ n⌉ bits — 256 lines into 8, a thousand into 10.

  • An encoder does the compression: 8 input lines in, the 3-bit number of the active line out. That's the 74x148.
  • A decoder reverses it: a 3-bit number in, exactly one of 8 output lines activated. That's the 74x237.

Both are pure combinational logic — no clock, no state (unlike Lesson 4), just gates computing which-line ↔ which-number.
intro.encoders_decoders.why_encode.title: Why Encode? Lines vs Bits
intro.finite_state_machines.description: Flip-flops remember which step a circuit is on; logic gates decide which step comes next. Learn the pattern behind traffic lights and CPUs, then crack a 3-button combination lock built from it.
How to Build One: the Skeleton

Every FSM, no matter how fancy, is the same two blocks wired in a loop — both of which you already know how to build:

  • A state register — flip-flops (Lesson 4) holding a code for the current state. Two flip-flops can hold 4 states, three can hold 8, and so on.
  • Next-state logic — plain combinational gates (Lesson 2) that look at the current state and the inputs, and compute which state to load next.

The magic is the feedback path: the register's outputs loop back around to become inputs of the gates. That's how "press 2" can mean different things — the gates see the button and the current state, so 1 then 2 takes a different path through the logic than 2 alone.

It's the same trick as the SR latch's cross-coupled feedback, scaled up: instead of a loop trapping one bit, it's a loop steering a whole machine.

Outputs (the LEDs here) usually just decode the state register — "state = OPEN, light the green LED". Machines wired that way are called Moore machines; if outputs also depend directly on the inputs, it's a Mealy machine. Same skeleton either way.
intro.finite_state_machines.lock_break.c:
Now Try to Break It

A state machine is defined as much by the transitions it refuses as the ones it takes. Attack the lock and watch the state diagram defend itself:

  • Press 3 first. Nothing. The AND gate guarding stage 3 sees stage 2's Q is LOW and swallows the press.
  • Press 1, then 3. The wrong-press detector fires — button 3 AND NOT stage 2 — and resets your progress. Finishing with 2, 3 afterwards won't unlock it.
  • Press 1, then wait out the yellow LED. Timeout: same reset line, back to LOCKED.

However you scramble the buttons, the machine is always in exactly one of its four states, and only the drawn arrows are possible. That predictability is why FSMs run everything from elevator doors to the instruction decoder in a CPU: you can list every state, every transition, and prove nothing else can happen.

When you've made the lock ignore or reset you at least once, click Next.
intro.finite_state_machines.lock_open.co:
Try It: Crack the Lock

Enter the combination:

  1. Press button 1 — the yellow LED lights: the 5 second window is open and you're in state GOT 1.
  2. Press button 2, then button 3, before the yellow LED goes out.
  3. The green UNLOCK LED lights — the machine walked LOCKED → GOT 1 → GOT 1,2 → OPEN.

Watch the yellow LED as you go. When the window expires, the lock snaps back to LOCKED on its own — even from OPEN. A state machine never stops; it just keeps taking transitions.

Complete this step by lighting the green UNLOCK LED. Too slow? Just press 1 and start again — resetting to a known state is exactly what the machine is built to do.
intro.finite_state_machines.lock_open.hi: 3) …then this, before the yellow LED dies
intro.finite_state_machines.lock_tour.co:
The Lock, Chip by Chip

On the board is the combination lock from the state diagram, built from chips you've already met. Find each block of the skeleton:

  • State register — the two CD4013s (middle). Three D flip-flops, one per step of progress: got 1, got 1,2, and OPEN. Each D pin is tied HIGH, so clocking a stage latches "reached".
  • Next-state logic — the CD4081 AND gates. Button 1 clocks stage 1 directly, but button 2 only reaches stage 2's clock through an AND with stage 1's Q — press 2 AND already got 1. Same again for button 3.
  • Wrong-press detector — the CD4069 NOT + CD4071 OR. Button 2 AND NOT stage 1 means you pressed out of order; the OR gate collects every such mistake and drives the reset pin of all three flip-flops. That's the red dashed arrow from the state diagram, in silicon.
  • The timer — the 74x4538 (far left). Pressing button 1 starts a real RC one-shot: the capacitor charges through the 100 kΩ resistor, giving a ~5 second window (the yellow LED). When it closes, it pulses the same reset line.

The LEDs just report the state: green = OPEN (stage 3's Q), red = LOCKED (its Q̄ — the built-in opposite), yellow = timer window.

Note the reset pulse also fires once at power-up — so the machine always boots in a known state, LOCKED. Every real FSM needs a defined starting state.
intro.finite_state_machines.quiz_guard.c:
Quiz 3 of 3

Fresh from power-up, you press button 3 and nothing at all happens. Why?
intro.finite_state_machines.quiz_next_st:
Quiz 1 of 3

What determines a state machine's next state?
intro.finite_state_machines.quiz_registe:
Quiz 2 of 3

In the combination lock, which chips form the state register — the part that remembers how far you've got?
intro.finite_state_machines.title: Lesson 13: Finite State Machines
Circuits With a Plan

In Lesson 4 you built circuits that remember one bit — a latch holds a value after the button that set it is released. But most useful machines need to remember something richer than one bit: they need to remember where they are in a sequence.

Think about a combination lock with the code 1 · 2 · 3. Pressing button 2 should do completely different things depending on history:

  • If you already pressed 1 — great, you're making progress.
  • If you pressed nothing yet — that's a wrong guess, start over.

Same input, different response. The circuit must be keeping track of which step it is on. That "which step" is called the state, and a circuit that moves between a fixed set of states in response to inputs is a finite state machine (FSM).

This diagram is the whole design of the lock, drawn before touching a single chip. Four states, three forward transitions, and a reset arrow for every way to get it wrong. Engineers draw this picture first — the circuit is just a translation of it into hardware.

"Finite" simply means the list of states is fixed and countable — this machine has exactly four. Your washing machine, an elevator controller, and the control unit inside a CPU are all FSMs with longer lists.
intro.glitches_hazards.board_intro.conte:
The Hazard, On the Bench

That exact selector is on the board: a 74x08 provides the two AND gates, a 74x32 the OR, and both data inputs are wired straight to VCC (A = B = 1). The green LED (column 33) shows Y — lit, as it should be.

One honest confession first: 74Sim's engine is idealized — it settles every net instantly, with no propagation delay. A real 10 ns glitch can't happen here on its own. So we'll cheat time: the inverter has been removed and replaced with a second switch.

  • Left switch (column 0) — SEL itself.
  • Right switch (column 2) — what the inverter's output should be. You are the inverter now, and your fingers are gloriously slow.

There's one more player: a CD4013 flip-flop wired as a toggle (Q̄ feeding D — the coin-toss trick from Lesson 4), with its clock pin connected to Y. The red LED (column 36) shows its state. Remember which state it's in.

Current state: SEL is ON, the inverter stand-in is OFF — consistent, since a real inverter outputs the opposite of its input. Y is HIGH. All quiet.
intro.glitches_hazards.board_intro.title: The Hazard, On the Bench
intro.glitches_hazards.description: Gates take time to answer. When two signal paths race, the output can blip a wrong value — a glitch. Freeze one in slow motion and watch a flip-flop count it as real.
intro.glitches_hazards.finish_swap.conte:
The Inverter Catches Up

Now play the inverter's late reply: turn the right switch ON.

The green LED comes back — the B gate has taken over, and Y is HIGH again, exactly as the logic equation always promised. If you looked away for a second, you'd swear nothing ever happened.

But check the red LED. It flipped.

The flip-flop's clock input can't tell the difference between a real clock edge and a glitch's recovery edge — an edge is an edge. Your "harmless" blip just got counted as an event by every clocked circuit listening to Y. In a counter that's a phantom count; in a register, a corrupted value; in a state machine, a wrong turn.
intro.glitches_hazards.finish_swap.title: The Inverter Catches Up
intro.glitches_hazards.fixes.content:
How Designers Fight Back

Fix 1 — cover the gap with redundant logic. You just proved the glitch disappears if something holds Y HIGH during the handoff. A third AND gate computing A·B does exactly that, automatically:

Logically the extra term is redundant — it never changes what Y is supposed to be. It exists purely to bridge the transition. (In Karnaugh-map terms it's called a consensus term: it covers the boundary the two original terms meet at.)

Fix 2 — the one the whole industry runs on: synchronous design. Patching every hazard gate-by-gate doesn't scale to millions of gates. Instead, designers surrender: let combinational logic glitch all it wants, but nobody listens until it has settled. Every flip-flop samples its input only on the shared clock edge, and the clock period is chosen longer than the slowest path's worst-case settling time. Glitches still happen — they just happen while no one is looking.

This is why the golden rule exists: never use a combinational output as a clock. Our demo board commits that sin on purpose — Y drives the flip-flop's clock pin directly, which is precisely why the glitch got counted.

Lesson 5's Schmitt trigger fought noise on a slow analog edge; hazards are wrongness born inside perfectly clean digital logic. Different disease, same symptom: false edges. The clock cures both.
intro.glitches_hazards.fixes.title: How Designers Fight Back
intro.glitches_hazards.make_glitch.conte:
Step Into the Glitch

Time to flip SEL from 1 to 0 — the handoff from the A gate to the B gate. In real hardware the inverter would follow 10 ns behind. You'll take considerably longer.

  1. Turn the left switch (SEL) OFF.
  2. Do not touch the right switch yet.

The green Y LED goes dark. Look at what you've built: SEL says "use B", but the inverter hasn't answered yet, so the B gate is still disabled. Both AND gates are outputting 0. You are standing inside the glitch.

On a real board this moment lasts nanoseconds. Here it lasts until you move your hand.
intro.glitches_hazards.make_glitch.title: Step Into the Glitch
intro.glitches_hazards.overlap_swap.cont:
Now Swap Without the Glitch

Swap back to SEL = 1, but this time reverse the order — turn the new gate on before turning the old one off:

  1. Turn the left switch (SEL) ON first. For a moment both switches are on — both AND gates enabled, both saying 1.
  2. Then turn the right switch OFF.

The green LED never blinks, and the red counter doesn't move. Same start point, same end point — but the handoff overlapped instead of leaving a gap, so Y was held HIGH the whole way through.

An overlap instead of a gap. Keep that idea; it's the heart of the classic fix on the next page.
intro.glitches_hazards.prop_delay.conten:
Gates Take Time

So far you've treated logic gates as instant: change an input, the output updates. Real silicon doesn't work that way. When an input changes, the new value has to charge and discharge the transistors inside the gate before the output pin moves.

That lag is called propagation delay (tpd) — around 10 nanoseconds for a classic LS-family gate. Ten billionths of a second sounds like nothing, but it has two consequences that shape all of digital design:

  • Chain gates together and the delays add up — a signal through five gates arrives five gate-delays late.
  • Send one signal down two different paths and the copies arrive at different times.

That second one is where the trouble lives. This lesson is about what happens in the gap.
intro.glitches_hazards.prop_delay.title: Gates Take Time
intro.glitches_hazards.quiz_cause.conten:
Quiz 1 of 3

What causes a combinational circuit to glitch during an input change?
intro.glitches_hazards.quiz_cause.title: Quiz 1 of 3
intro.glitches_hazards.quiz_counter.cont:
Quiz 2 of 3

Y glitched LOW for 20 nanoseconds and came right back. Why did the flip-flop wired to Y toggle anyway?
intro.glitches_hazards.quiz_fix.content:
Quiz 3 of 3

Which habit protects a large design from hazards without hunting them down one by one?
intro.glitches_hazards.quiz_fix.title: Quiz 3 of 3
intro.glitches_hazards.timing.content:
Anatomy of a Glitch

Watch the handoff in slow motion. SEL falls from 1 to 0. The top AND turns off immediately. But the bottom AND can't turn on until the inverter finishes — one gate-delay later.

In that window both AND gates output 0 at once, so Y drops LOW for a few nanoseconds before recovering. The unwanted pulse is a glitch; the circuit flaw that permits it is the hazard.

This flavour — output supposed to sit at 1, briefly dips to 0 — is a static-1 hazard. (Its mirror image, a 0 that blips HIGH, is a static-0 hazard.)

Vocabulary worth keeping straight: the hazard is the design property ("this circuit can glitch"); the glitch is the event ("there it went"). Datasheets and textbooks use both.
intro.glitches_hazards.timing.title: Anatomy of a Glitch
intro.glitches_hazards.title: Lesson 6: Glitches & Hazards
intro.glitches_hazards.two_paths.content:
Two Paths Race

Here's a 2-way selector: when SEL is 1, output Y follows input A; when SEL is 0, Y follows input B. One AND gate per input, an OR to combine them, and an inverter so only one AND is enabled at a time: Y = A·SEL + B·SEL̄.

Notice that SEL reaches the two AND gates by paths of different length: the top AND gets it directly, the bottom AND gets it through the inverter — one extra gate-delay later.

Now set both data inputs HIGH (A = B = 1). Whichever way SEL points, Y should be 1. Flipping SEL just hands the job from one AND gate to the other — the output has no reason to change.

A circuit where unequal delays can produce a momentary wrong output is said to have a hazard. Whether it actually misbehaves comes down to a race measured in nanoseconds.
intro.glitches_hazards.two_paths.title: Two Paths Race
intro.latches_flipflops.description: Meet the first circuit that remembers: cross-coupled gates form a latch, and a clocked flip-flop stores one bit per tick.
intro.latches_flipflops.dff_board.conten:
Try It: the Coin Toss

This board uses half of a CD4013 dual D flip-flop, wired as the toggle from the last page: Q̄ (pin 2) feeds back into D (pin 5), so every clock edge flips the state. Green LED = HEADS (Q), red LED = TAILS (Q̄) — always opposites.

A 240 Hz clock is on the board, but it only reaches the CLK pin while you hold the TOSS button:

  1. Hold TOSS. The flip-flop toggles 240 times a second — far faster than your eye can follow, both LEDs blur.
  2. Release. The last clock edge wins, and the latch holds it: heads or tails, genuinely unpredictable.

Notice the supporting cast, all ideas you already know: a 10 k pull-down keeps CLK at a clean LOW while the button is open (no floating input, no stray edges), and the CD4013's SET/RESET pins are tied to GND to keep them out of the way.

Complete this step by holding TOSS for a moment, then releasing — flip a few coins.
intro.latches_flipflops.dff_board.title: D Flip-Flop on the Board
intro.latches_flipflops.dff_theory.conte:
Adding a Clock

The SR latch reacts the instant an input changes. That's fine for buttons, but inside a computer millions of stored bits must all update in lockstep, or the machine falls apart. The fix: only let the latch change at one agreed moment — the tick of a clock.

A D flip-flop wraps a latch behind edge-detecting logic. It has a single data input, D, and a clock input, CLK:

  • On each rising edge of CLK (the instant it goes LOW→HIGH), Q takes a snapshot of whatever D is at that moment.
  • At every other time — D can change all it likes — Q holds the snapshot.

The little triangle on the CLK pin in the symbol is how datasheets say "edge-triggered". One flip-flop stores one bit per tick; put 8 side by side sharing a clock and you have a register holding a byte.

One classic trick: wire Q̄ back to D. Every clock edge then loads the opposite of the current state — the flip-flop toggles, dividing the clock frequency by 2. You're about to see it.
intro.latches_flipflops.dff_theory.title: From Latch to Flip-Flop

intro.latches_flipflops.sr_latch_board.c:
Try It: a Real SR Latch

On the board is a CD4001 — four NOR gates in one package, two of them cross-wired exactly like the diagram. The SET button (right, column 24) drives S; the RESET button (left, column 20) drives R. Green LED = Q, red LED = Q̄.

  1. Press and release SET. The green Q LED turns on — and stays on. The circuit remembers the press.
  2. Press and release RESET. Q goes out, the red Q̄ LED takes over — and holds.
  3. With both buttons released the latch is in HOLD: poke nothing, and it keeps its bit forever.

Note the 10 kΩ pull-down resistor under each button — the same rule you learned in Lesson 1. A CMOS input must never float, and HOLD only works if a released button reads a clean LOW.

Complete this step by pressing SET so the green Q LED latches ON.
The SR Latch

Take two NOR gates and cross-wire them: each gate's output feeds one input of the other. The two free inputs are called S (set) and R (reset).

A NOR gate outputs HIGH only when both inputs are LOW. Work through the loop and four behaviours fall out:

  • S=1, R=0 — SET. Q is forced HIGH, and stays HIGH.
  • S=0, R=1 — RESET. Q is forced LOW, and stays LOW.
  • S=0, R=0 — HOLD. Neither input interferes; the feedback loop keeps whatever Q already was. This is the memory.
  • S=1, R=1 — forbidden. Both outputs are forced LOW at once (Q and Q̄ are supposed to be opposites), and when the inputs release, the loop lands unpredictably. Real designs avoid this input.
Q̄ ("Q-bar") is the complement output — always the opposite of Q. Having both available for free is a side effect of the cross-coupled structure.
intro.latches_flipflops.title: Lesson 4: Latches & Flip-Flops
intro.latches_flipflops.what_is_state.co:
Circuits That Remember

Everything you have built so far is combinational: the output depends only on what the inputs are right now. Release the buttons on the AND-gate board and the LED goes out — the circuit has no idea the buttons were ever pressed.

Sequential circuits are different. Their output depends on inputs and on what happened before. They have state — a stored value that persists after the input that created it is gone.

The trick that makes this possible is feedback: route a gate's output back around to its own input, and the circuit's present output becomes part of its own future input. The loop can hold itself in a condition indefinitely.

Every kind of memory in a computer — registers, cache, RAM — is built on this one idea. In this lesson you'll meet the two simplest forms:

  • The SR latch — two gates in a feedback loop, set and reset directly
  • The D flip-flop — a latch that only updates when a clock ticks
intro.memory_intro.board_mem_intro.conte:
Try It: ROM and RAM, Same Address

The two ADDRESS switches (bottom left) feed both chips at once, selecting location 0–3 in each. Red LEDs show the ROM's word at that address; green LEDs show the RAM's.

  1. Step through the addresses. The ROM (a 28C16 EEPROM, wired read-only) has data baked in: address 1 holds 6, address 2 holds 9, address 3 holds 15. The RAM shows nothing anywhere — it's fresh out of power-on, so it's empty. That blankness is volatility, live on the board.
  2. Press COPY at some address. The button pulses the RAM's write-enable, storing the ROM's current word into the RAM at the same address — the green LEDs snap to match the red.
  3. Walk away and come back. Change the address, then return: the RAM kept what you copied. Copy all four locations and the two chips match everywhere.
Copying ROM into RAM at startup is exactly what real computers do — it's called shadowing, and you just did it by hand. Complete this step by finding some data (flip an address switch).
intro.memory_intro.board_mem_intro.title: Both on One Board
intro.memory_intro.description: Two ways to store many bits: ROM you read but can't change in circuit, RAM you read and write but loses it on power-off. Both on one board.

intro.memory_intro.rom_vs_ram.content:
The Two Families

Memory chips split by one question: can the circuit change the contents, and do they survive power-off?

ROM — Read-Only Memory. The contents are put in ahead of time and the circuit can only read them. In exchange, they're non-volatile: unplug it for a year, the data is still there. That's where a computer's first instructions live — the machine has to know what to do before it could possibly have loaded anything.

RAM — Random-Access Memory. The circuit can read and write any cell at any moment. The price: it's volatile. The cells are just powered latches, and when the power drops, every bit evaporates. RAM always wakes up blank.

"Random access" just means any address is equally quick to reach — as opposed to a shift register (Lesson 8), where bits arrive in order and you wait your turn.
intro.memory_intro.rom_vs_ram.title: ROM vs RAM
intro.memory_intro.title: Lesson 10: Intro to Memory — RAM vs ROM
intro.memory_intro.what_memory_is.conten:
From One Bit to Many

Lesson 4's latch stores one bit. A useful memory stores thousands — so how do you talk to thousands of latches without a wire to each one? You give every group of them a number, called an address, and build the selection right into the chip:

Present an address, and a decoder — the very circuit from Lesson 7 — activates exactly one row of storage cells. That row's contents appear on the data pins. Every memory chip, from this board's little 16×4 RAM to the gigabytes in your PC, is this same picture scaled up:

  • Address pins — say which word you want. n address bits reach 2ⁿ words.
  • Data pins — carry the word itself.
  • Control pins — say what to do with it (read it out, or store a new one).
intro.memory_intro.what_memory_is.title: What Memory Is
intro.push_pull_intro.description: Understand how charge is pushed and pulled on a net, and why output type matters in real hardware.
intro.push_pull_intro.intro.content:
Pushing and Pulling Charge

In a pure digital system, an active input produces a HIGH, and when it's released the signal returns to LOW automatically. Clean and simple.

Real hardware is different. When voltage is applied to a net, charge builds up in the parasitic capacitance of the traces, pins, and components on that net.

When the voltage source is removed, that charge doesn't disappear — it stays trapped, keeping the net logically HIGH. Without a path to ground, the net floats at whatever charge remains.

This gives us two fundamental operations:

  • Pushing charge — a path from a supply voltage to the net, driving it HIGH by charging the parasitic capacitance
  • Pulling charge — a path from the net to ground, draining that charge and driving the net LOW

Every output pin in digital hardware is built around some combination of these two operations. The four types are covered on the next pages.

In the simulator, nets default to 0 V — floating is modelled as LOW. Physical hardware is subtler, which is why output types matter.
intro.push_pull_intro.intro.title: Pushing and Pulling
intro.push_pull_intro.open_drain.content:
Open-Drain / Open-Collector

The output stage has only a transistor to ground. It can pull the net LOW, but it cannot actively drive it HIGH. To reach a HIGH state, an external pull-up resistor connected to the supply is required.

When the transistor turns off, the pull-up resistor slowly charges the net toward VCC. The speed of the HIGH transition depends on the resistor value and the net's capacitance.

Two important uses:

  • Wired-OR / Wired-AND — multiple open-drain outputs can share one wire without contention. Any device pulling LOW wins.
  • Voltage translation — the pull-up can be at a different voltage than the driving chip, allowing easy level shifting.
I²C (SDA and SCL) uses open-drain for exactly this reason — any device on the bus can pull the line low, and the pull-up restores it to idle-HIGH.
intro.push_pull_intro.open_drain.title: Open-Drain (Open-Collector)
intro.push_pull_intro.push_only.content:
Push-Only

A push-only output can drive the net HIGH but has no active path to ground. To bring the net back LOW, an external pull-down resistor must drain the charge.

Here the button acts as the push-only device. When pressed it connects VCC to the net, pushing charge in. When released, the resistor provides the only discharge path, slowly pulling the net back to GND.

Without the pull-down resistor the net would float HIGH indefinitely after being pressed once.

Push-only is rarely used inside a chip pin — it has no advantages over open-drain and is less power efficient. But it's a useful model for simple switch circuits.
intro.push_pull_intro.push_only.title: Push-Only Output
intro.push_pull_intro.push_pull.content:
Push-Pull (Totem Pole)

Push-pull uses two transistors — one connected to the supply, one to ground. It actively pushes to drive HIGH and actively pulls to drive LOW.

This is the standard output for most logic gates. Because both transitions are active, switching is fast and the output voltage is well-defined in both states.

You can model it as an SPDT switch: one throw connected to VCC, the other to GND, with the pole as the output. The lever can only sit in one position at a time — either pushing or pulling.

Most 74 series gates (74x08 AND, 74x04 NOT, 74x32 OR) have push-pull outputs. No external pull resistors needed.
intro.push_pull_intro.push_pull.title: Push-Pull (Totem Pole)

intro.push_pull_intro.quiz_intro.content:
Knowledge Check

Three short questions. Each one puts a real circuit on the board — read it and identify which output type the chip uses.

  • Push-Only — active path to VCC only; needs external pull-down
  • Open-Drain — active path to GND only; needs external pull-up
  • Push-Pull — active paths to both VCC and GND; fast transitions
  • Tri-State — push-pull plus a disconnected (Hi-Z) state via enable

Select the correct answer to move on.
intro.push_pull_intro.quiz_intro.title: Knowledge Check
intro.push_pull_intro.title: Lesson 1: Pushing and Pulling
intro.push_pull_intro.tri_state.content:
Tri-State (High-Impedance)

Tri-state extends push-pull with a third state: disconnected. The output can be driven HIGH, driven LOW, or put into a high-impedance (Hi-Z) state where it effectively disconnects from the net entirely.

In the SPDT model, the middle position leaves the pole floating — connected to neither VCC nor GND. The output sits at whatever voltage the rest of the net imposes.

This is what makes shared buses possible. In a CPU memory bus, for example, many chips are wired to the same data lines. Only one chip drives at a time; all others tri-state their outputs to avoid contention.

The OE (output enable) pin on many 74 series chips (74x244, 74x245 buffers) controls tri-state. Pull OE LOW to drive, pull it HIGH to go Hi-Z.
intro.push_pull_intro.tri_state.title: Tri-State (Hi-Z)
intro.ram_deep.board_ram.content:
Try It: the Full Memory Loop

Left to right under the chip: A0, D1, A1, D2 switches (address bits interleaved with data bits — labels on the board), the WRITE button above on the right, and two green LEDs showing the stored word at the current address.

  1. Pick a cell: set A0/A1, say both OFF (address 0). LEDs dark — empty, as always at power-on.
  2. Offer a value: flip D1 ON. LEDs still dark! Data inputs alone change nothing — the chip is in read mode.
  3. Hold WRITE. WE̅ goes LOW and the value is stored. Notice the LEDs stay dark while you hold it — the outputs are tri-stated mid-write.
  4. Release. Back in read mode: the LEDs now show your bit, read back from the cell.
  5. Prove it's really stored: change the address (LEDs go dark — different, empty cell), write something different there, then come back. Each cell kept its own value.
Complete this step by writing a non-zero value and reading it back (get a green LED lit from a stored bit).
intro.ram_deep.board_ram.hint.0.label: 1) flip D1 ON
intro.ram_deep.board_ram.hint.1.label: 2) hold WRITE, then release
intro.ram_deep.board_ram.hint.2.label: 3) the stored bit reads back
intro.ram_deep.board_ram.title: Write It, Then Read It Back
intro.ram_deep.description: Read/write volatile memory: address selects a cell, WE picks read or write, data flows both ways — and it forgets on power-off.

intro.ram_deep.ram_rw_paths.content:
One More Pin Changes Everything

Take Lesson 10's ROM read path and add a way in: that's RAM. The new control pin is WE̅ (write enable), and it steers the direction of the data:

  • WE̅ HIGH — read. The addressed cell's contents drive the outputs, exactly like the ROM.
  • WE̅ LOW — write. Whatever is on the data inputs is stored into the addressed cell, replacing what was there. During the write the outputs go tri-state (Lesson 1 again): the port can't carry data out while data is going in.

This board's chip is a 74x219: 16 words of 4 bits, each cell a pair of cross-coupled inverters — literally the latch from Lesson 4, times 64. Many memory chips go further and use the same physical pins for data in and data out (a bidirectional bus); the 74x219 keeps them separate, which makes the two directions easy to see.
intro.ram_deep.ram_rw_paths.title: Read and Write
intro.ram_deep.ram_volatile.content:
Volatility Is Not a Defect

Each RAM cell holds its bit the way Lesson 4's latch did: a feedback loop actively driving itself, powered every nanosecond. Cut the power and the loop isn't holding anything — it's just two dead transistors. When power returns, each loop falls whichever way manufacturing imperfections push it. That's why RAM wakes up as garbage and must be treated as blank.

So why tolerate a memory with amnesia? Because the same properties that make it volatile make it fast and endlessly rewritable — a latch flips in nanoseconds and never wears out, while an EEPROM write is thousands of times slower and each cell survives a limited number of erases.

Hence the division of labour in every computer since the beginning:

  • ROM / flash holds what must survive: firmware, the OS, your files.
  • RAM holds what's being worked on right now — copied in from storage (remember shadowing, in Lesson 9?), chewed on at full speed, written back if it's worth keeping.
intro.ram_deep.ram_volatile.title: Why It Forgets
intro.ram_deep.title: Lesson 12: RAM, Deeper
intro.rom_deep.board_rom.content:
Try It: a Chip That "Computes" Squares

This 28C16 was programmed so that location x stores x². The three ADDRESS switches form x (0–7); the eight red LEDs read out the stored byte in binary, 1s bit on the left.

  1. Set the switches to 5 (ON–OFF–ON, right-to-left: A0=1, A1=0, A2=1). The LEDs show 25 — bits 1, 8 and 16.
  2. Try 7: out comes 49. Try 0: all dark. Every answer in one address-time, no gates computing anything.

This is a lookup table, and it's a genuinely important trick: any function of n input bits can be "computed" by a 2ⁿ-word ROM that simply stores every answer in advance. Early arcade games drew graphics this way; digital synths store whole sine waves in ROM; and Lesson 7's decoder is really just a 1-bit-deep lookup table.

Complete this step by looking up any non-zero square (set the address to 1 or more).
intro.rom_deep.board_rom.hint.0.label: ADDRESS — set to 1 or more (A0 is rightmost)
intro.rom_deep.board_rom.hint.1.label: x² comes out here
intro.rom_deep.board_rom.title: ROM as a Lookup Table
intro.rom_deep.description: Non-volatile read-only memory: how an address selects a fixed data word, and why we use it for firmware and lookup tables.

Quiz 2 of 2

Quiz 1 of 2
