intro.rom_deep.quiz_oe.title: Quiz 1 of 2
intro.rom_deep.rom_programming.content:
Where the Bits Come From

"Read-only" means the circuit can't change it — somebody still had to put the data in. The family tree, one line each:

  • Mask ROM — the data is etched into the silicon at the factory. Cheapest per chip, unchangeable forever.
  • PROM — ships blank; a programmer burns microscopic fuses. One shot, no undo.
  • EPROM — erasable with 20 minutes of UV light through the little quartz window, then reprogrammable.
  • EEPROM — erasable electrically, byte by byte. This board's 28C16 is one.
  • Flash — EEPROM's fast, block-erased descendant: SSDs, USB sticks, your phone.

Notice the whole history is one long fight to make "read-only" easier to change — while still keeping the part that matters: the data survives with no power.

In this simulator the EEPROM's contents were simply preloaded into the saved circuit — the equivalent of the chip arriving from the programmer already burned.
intro.rom_deep.rom_programming.title: How ROM Gets Its Data
intro.rom_deep.rom_read_path.content:
Inside a Read

A ROM is the memory picture from Lesson 9 with the write machinery removed. This board's 28C16 stores 2048 bytes: 11 address pins (2¹¹ = 2048) select a byte, and 8 data pins deliver it.

Three control pins run the show, all active-low:

  • CE̅ (chip enable) — the master switch. HIGH = chip asleep, pins disconnected.
  • OE̅ (output enable) — the tri-state gate from Lesson 1. Only when OE̅ is LOW does the chip drive its data onto the pins; otherwise they go Hi-Z so other chips can share the same bus. A computer hangs its ROM, RAM and peripherals on one data bus and uses OE̅ lines to pick who talks.
  • WE̅ (write enable) — this chip is an EEPROM, so writing is electrically possible; on this board WE̅ is wired to VCC, so it behaves as pure ROM.
intro.rom_deep.rom_read_path.title: Address → Fixed Data
intro.rom_deep.title: Lesson 11: ROM, Deeper
intro.schmitt_triggers.description: A gate with hysteresis: two switching thresholds turn a slow, noisy signal into a clean digital edge — and make a simple RC oscillator.
intro.schmitt_triggers.hysteresis.conten:
The Fix: Two Thresholds

A Schmitt trigger is a gate with hysteresis: instead of one threshold it has two.

  • A rising input doesn't flip the output until it climbs above the upper threshold VT+.
  • A falling input doesn't flip it back until it drops below the lower threshold VT−.

After the output flips, the input would have to backtrack all the way across the gap between the thresholds to flip it again. Noise smaller than that gap does nothing: one crossing, one clean edge.

The distinctive "hysteresis loop" glyph inside the gate symbol marks a Schmitt input. The 74x14 is the classic part: six Schmitt-trigger inverters in one package.

Hysteresis has a bonus superpower: because the gate flips at two different voltages, it can turn a smoothly drifting analog voltage into an oscillation. Next page.
intro.schmitt_triggers.hysteresis.title: Hysteresis: Two Thresholds
intro.schmitt_triggers.noisy_edges.conte:
Real Signals Are Ugly

Logic diagrams draw signals as perfect square edges. Real inputs often aren't: a capacitor charging, a sensor warming up, a long wire picking up interference, a mechanical switch bouncing — all of these cross the logic threshold slowly, with noise on top.

An ordinary gate has one threshold voltage. While a slow input crawls through that threshold, every little noise wiggle re-crosses it — and the output chatters, firing a burst of false edges instead of one clean transition.

Feed that chattering output into a counter or a flip-flop (Lesson 4!) and it will count every false edge. One button press becomes fifteen.
intro.schmitt_triggers.noisy_edges.title: The Problem: Slow, Noisy Edges
intro.schmitt_triggers.quiz_freq.content:
Quiz 2 of 2

In the RC oscillator you just watched, what sets the blink frequency?
intro.schmitt_triggers.quiz_freq.title: Quiz 2 of 2
intro.schmitt_triggers.quiz_why_two.cont:
Quiz 1 of 2

Why does a Schmitt trigger ignore noise that an ordinary gate would chatter on?
intro.schmitt_triggers.rc_oscillator.con:
Watch It: a One-Gate Clock

On the board, a 74x14 Schmitt inverter has its output fed back to its own input through a resistor, with a capacitor from the input to GND. That's the whole oscillator — and the LED is blinking to prove it.

Follow one cycle:

  1. Say the cap is discharged: input LOW → inverter output HIGH → the output charges the cap through the resistor.
  2. The cap voltage creeps up... past VT−... nothing happens (hysteresis!)... until it crosses VT+ — the output snaps LOW.
  3. Now the output discharges the cap back down until it crosses VT− — the output snaps HIGH again. Repeat forever.

The cap shuttles between the two thresholds, and the output is a square wave. The frequency is set by how fast the RC pair moves the cap between VT− and VT+: bigger R or bigger C → slower clock.

An ordinary inverter could not do this: with a single threshold there is no gap to shuttle across, and the loop just settles at the threshold and sits there.

A second 74x14 gate buffers the oscillator to the LED so the blinking load doesn't disturb the timing node.
intro.schmitt_triggers.title: Lesson 5: Schmitt Triggers
intro.serial_piso_sipo.board_piso.conten:
Try It: Send a Byte Across

Two separate breadboards. LEFT: 8 switches and the 74x165. RIGHT: the 74x164 and 8 green LEDs. Count the wires crossing between the boards: serial data and clock — that's it (plus the shared rails).

You are the clock generator. Send a byte by hand:

  1. Set a pattern on the 8 switches (a few are already ON).
  2. Press Load/Reset (bottom button) — it snapshots the switches into the 165 and clears the 164.
  3. Press the Serial Load clock button 8 times — and watch your pattern crawl across the LEDs, one bit per press, until after the 8th press it sits in place, matching the switches.

That crawl you're watching is the serial stream itself — normally it happens millions of times a second, invisibly.

Change the switches and send another byte. Note: changing switches alone does nothing until you Load — the 165's flip-flops hold the old snapshot. That's Lesson 4's HOLD state doing real work.
intro.serial_piso_sipo.board_piso.title: The Link on the Board
intro.serial_piso_sipo.description: Send 8 bits over 1 wire and time. A PISO shift register turns parallel data into a serial stream; a SIPO turns it back.
intro.serial_piso_sipo.quiz_clock_role.c:
Quiz 2 of 2

Why did the byte need exactly 8 clock presses to arrive?
intro.serial_piso_sipo.quiz_wires.conten:
Quiz 1 of 2

intro.serial_piso_sipo.quiz_wires.title: Quiz 1 of 2
intro.serial_piso_sipo.shift_registers.c:
The Hardware: Chained Flip-Flops

The machine that converts between "8 bits side by side" and "8 bits one after another" is the shift register: 8 D flip-flops (Lesson 4!) sharing one clock, each one's Q wired to the next one's D. Every clock edge, the whole row of bits steps one position sideways.

Two flavours make a transmitter and a receiver:

  • 74x165 — PISO (parallel in, serial out). A LOAD pin snapshots all 8 inputs into the flip-flops at once; then each clock tick pushes the next bit out the end of the chain.
  • 74x164 — SIPO (serial in, parallel out). Each clock tick shifts one incoming bit into the chain; after 8 ticks the whole byte sits on its 8 output pins.

Both ends share the clock wire, so they step in perfect lockstep — and they must share GND too, or "HIGH" and "LOW" mean nothing between the boards (voltages are always measured against a common reference).
intro.serial_piso_sipo.title: Lesson 9: Serial Communication
intro.serial_piso_sipo.wires_vs_time.con:
Trading Wires for Time

Lesson 8 shrank 8 lines to 3 by encoding which single line is active. But what if all 8 bits matter at once — a whole byte of data? Encoding can't compress that; 8 independent bits genuinely need 8 wires...

...or 1 wire used 8 times.

That's the whole idea of serial communication: send the bits one after another down a single wire, one per clock tick, and reassemble them at the far end. It's slower — 8 ticks instead of 1 — but the cable shrinks from 8 wires to basically 2 (data + clock).

Almost every cable you own works this way: USB, HDMI, SATA, Ethernet. Wires are expensive; ticks are nearly free.
intro.sim_basics.an_circuit.content:
Analysis 1 of 3: the Circuit Analyzer

Your OR gate works — now meet the tools that show you why a circuit works (or doesn't). In the full simulator they live in the Analysis ▾ menu at the top right. First up: the Circuit Analyzer.

It's the electrical view: every net's live voltage, every resistor's and LED's current, and the total power draw. Click any row and the matching net lights up on the board.

Reach for it when something is electrically wrong: an LED that stays dark (is its net even getting voltage?), a part running hot (over-current rows get flagged), or the big one — a short-circuit warning when VCC and GND touch.

Try pressing a button on your board and imagine the table updating — the LED row jumping from ○ OFF to ● LIT with ~4 mA through it.
intro.sim_basics.an_circuit.title: Analysis 1 of 3: Circuit Analyzer
intro.sim_basics.an_logic.content:
Analysis 2 of 3: the Combinational Logic Analyzer

The second panel ignores volts entirely and reads your circuit as Boolean logic.

It names every input and output automatically (buttons become B1, B2…, LEDs become LED1…), then works out the expression connecting them. For the board you just wired it would derive LED1 = B1 + B2 — plus a full truth table and a Karnaugh map for every output.

Its state list is clickable, too: toggle switches from the panel and watch the outputs respond. Use it to check that what you built matches what you meant — invaluable once circuits grow past one gate.

"+" means OR in Boolean notation, not addition — a format dropdown switches between programming style (a*b+c), math symbols (∧ ∨) and plain AND/OR words.
intro.sim_basics.an_logic.title: Analysis 2 of 3: Logic Analyzer
intro.sim_basics.an_timing.content:
Analysis 3 of 3: Timing Analysis

The third panel answers when. Real gates aren't instant — a 74LS32 takes roughly 14–22 nanoseconds to change its output. Timing Analysis replays your circuit event by event using each chip's real propagation delay and draws the result as waveforms.

You get transport controls — Run, Step (advance to the next event), and ↺ t=0 — plus a speed dial from real time down to nanoseconds-per-second slow motion. To watch a signal, drop a Test Point on its net (More ▾ → Test Point in the full simulator) and it appears as a lane.

This is the panel for the subtle stuff: race conditions, glitches (it can flag pulses shorter than a threshold you set), and understanding why fast circuits misbehave in ways a truth table can't explain.

That's the tour. You can wire a chip, read the numbers, and you know where the x-ray tools live. Hit Finish Lesson — next up: what HIGH and LOW actually are, with charge, pushing and pulling.

Want to keep playing? Open the full simulator from the home page any time — your lesson progress stays here.
intro.sim_basics.an_timing.title: Analysis 3 of 3: Timing Analysis
intro.sim_basics.breadboard.content:
How a Breadboard Works

A breadboard looks like a field of unrelated holes, but inside it is full of hidden metal strips. The red lines on the board right now are an x-ray of those strips: every hole touching the same red line is already connected — no wire needed.

Three things to spot:

  • Column strips — in the main grid, each column of 5 holes (rows A–E on top, F–J on the bottom) is one strip. Plug two things into the same 5-hole column and they're joined.
  • The center channel — the gap between the two halves. Nothing crosses it. Chips sit across the channel so every pin lands on its own private strip.
  • Power rails — the long + and − rows along the top and bottom. Each rail row is one strip running the whole board, even across the small cosmetic gaps drawn every 5 holes. In 74Sim the + rail is the 5 V supply and the − rail is ground (0 V) — there's no separate battery part.
The red x-ray is lesson-only — the real simulator doesn't draw it. One more detail: this board is built from tiles, and a rail stops at its tile's edge. If you ever work across multiple tiles, bridge the rails with a wire, exactly like on a real bench.
intro.sim_basics.breadboard.title: How a Breadboard Works
intro.sim_basics.build_intro.content:
Your First Chip: a 74x32 OR Gate

Time to build. The board now has the supporting cast already placed:

  • Button A (column 8) and button B (column 12) — each one puts 5 V on its strip while pressed; a pull-down resistor keeps the strip at 0 V otherwise
  • A red LED (column 30) with its current-limiting resistor to ground

You'll add a 74x32 — a chip containing four independent 2-input OR gates — and wire one gate so the LED lights when either button is pressed.

In the full simulator you find parts yourself: Chip Search ▾ to search any 74-series chip by number or by what it does, Input ▾ for buttons and switches, Output ▾ for LEDs and displays. In this lesson the toolbar above the board offers exactly the tools each step needs.

The pre-placed parts are locked so you can't accidentally drag them — but buttons still press just fine.
intro.sim_basics.build_intro.title: Your First Chip
intro.sim_basics.description: Learn the simulator itself: how a breadboard secretly connects holes, what wire numbers mean, how to place and power your first chip (a 74x32 OR gate), and what the three Analysis panels tell you.
intro.sim_basics.nets.content:
Wire Numbers & Nets

In 74Sim a wire is not drawn as a line. Each wire is just two dots — one on each hole it connects — stamped with a number. That keeps big circuits readable instead of burying the board in spaghetti.

The rule: dots with the same number are the same electrical connection, no matter how far apart they sit. Everything joined together — strips, wires, pins — forms one net, and the number is that net's name. Dots on the same net also share a color.

Look at the board:

  • Three wires carry the number 0. They never touch visually, but each one ends on a strip the next one starts on — one net, one number.
  • The purple pair marked 1 is a separate net.
  • The endpoints on the + rail say VCC instead of a number (ground connections say GND). A red ring around a dot means that net is currently HIGH.

Try it: press and hold the push button. Watch the LED light up through the whole numbered chain.

Numbers are assigned automatically as you wire — you never have to manage them. Right-click any wire dot to see that jumper's live voltage and current.
intro.sim_basics.nets.hint.0.label: Press and hold this button
intro.sim_basics.nets.hint.1.label: LED — fed through the net-0 chain
intro.sim_basics.nets.title: Wire Numbers & Nets
intro.sim_basics.place_chip.content:
Place the Chip

Click Place 74x32 in the toolbar, then click the breadboard to drop the chip. Place it around columns 18–24, straddling the center channel.

Chips always sit across the channel: the top row of pins lands on row E, the bottom row on row F, and every pin gets its own 5-hole strip to connect to.

Pin numbering is the same on every DIP chip: pin 1 is bottom-left, numbers run counterclockwise — along the bottom to pin 7, then back along the top to pin 14 at top-left.

Misplaced it? Press Escape to cancel a ghost, or drag the placed chip to move it. Hover any pin to see its name.
intro.sim_basics.place_chip.hint.0.label: Place the chip here, across the channel
intro.sim_basics.place_chip.title: Place the 74x32
intro.sim_basics.test_or.content:
Test It!

The circuit is complete. Prove it ORs:

  1. Press button A — the LED lights
  2. Press button B instead — the LED lights
  3. Hold both — still lit
  4. Release everything — off

That's the OR truth table, live: the output is HIGH when at least one input is HIGH. One press of a button and you've used a real logic chip.

In Lesson 2 you'll wire this exact same board to an AND gate, which lights only when both buttons are down. Same wiring, different chip — that's the 74-series in a nutshell.
intro.sim_basics.test_or.hint.0.label: Press A…
intro.sim_basics.test_or.hint.1.label: …or B — either lights it
intro.sim_basics.test_or.hint.2.label: LED
intro.sim_basics.test_or.title: Test It!
intro.sim_basics.title: Lesson 0: Welcome to 74Sim
intro.sim_basics.welcome.content:
Welcome to 74Sim

74Sim is a virtual breadboard for real 74-series logic chips. You place chips, buttons and LEDs on the board, connect them, and the circuit behaves like the physical parts would — voltages, currents, propagation delays and all.

This lesson is a tour of the tool itself. You will:

  • See how a breadboard secretly connects some holes to each other
  • Learn what the numbers on wires mean (this is the #1 thing that confuses new users)
  • Place and power your first chip — a 74x32 OR gate — and make it do something
  • Meet the three Analysis panels that let you look inside a running circuit

Two controls worth knowing before we start: zoom with the scroll wheel, and pan by dragging with the middle mouse button (or hold Alt and drag).

No electronics background needed — later lessons cover the theory. This one is just about finding your way around.
intro.sim_basics.welcome.title: Welcome
intro.sim_basics.wire_a.content:
Button A → Pin 1 (1A)

Feed the first OR input. Wire from any free hole on button A's strip (column 8, rows A–E) to pin 1 (1A) — the bottom-left pin of the chip.

This is the breadboard trick from earlier doing real work: the button, its pull-down and your new wire all share the column-8 strip, so they're all one net.

The chip's first gate reads its two inputs on pins 1 and 2 (1A, 1B) and drives its result on pin 3 (1Y).
intro.sim_basics.wire_a.hint.0.label: button A's strip (col 8)
intro.sim_basics.wire_a.hint.1.label: pin 1 (1A)
intro.sim_basics.wire_a.title: Wire Button A → Input 1A
intro.sim_basics.wire_b.content:
Button B → Pin 2 (1B)

Same again for the second input: wire from button B's strip (column 12) to pin 2 (1B), the second pin on the bottom row.

Unconnected inputs are not "off" — they float, and floating inputs misbehave on real chips. Later lessons make a whole story of this; for now, both gate inputs get a definite signal.
intro.sim_basics.wire_b.hint.0.label: button B's strip (col 12)
intro.sim_basics.wire_b.hint.1.label: pin 2 (1B)
intro.sim_basics.wire_b.title: Wire Button B → Input 1B
intro.sim_basics.wire_gnd.content:
Connect Ground: Pin 7 → −

Now the return path: pin 7 (GND) — the bottom-right pin — goes to the − rail at the bottom of the board.

Same move as before: Wire, click pin 7, click the − rail.

VCC top-left, GND bottom-right is the standard layout for most 14-pin logic chips — you'll stop thinking about it very quickly.
intro.sim_basics.wire_gnd.hint.0.label: pin 7 (GND)
intro.sim_basics.wire_gnd.hint.1.label: − rail
intro.sim_basics.wire_gnd.title: Connect Ground (GND)
intro.sim_basics.wire_led.content:
Pin 3 (1Y) → LED

Last wire: connect the gate's output, pin 3 (1Y), to the LED's anode strip — column 30, any free hole on rows F–J below the channel.

Current will flow from the pin, through the LED, through its resistor, into the − rail — and the LED will light whenever 1Y drives HIGH.

The resistor limits the LED current to a safe few milliamps. Drive an LED without one and the Circuit Analyzer (two steps from now) will show you exactly why that's a bad idea.
intro.sim_basics.wire_led.hint.0.label: pin 3 (1Y, output)
intro.sim_basics.wire_led.hint.1.label: LED anode strip (col 30)
intro.sim_basics.wire_led.title: Wire the Output → LED
intro.sim_basics.wire_vcc.content:
Connect Power: Pin 14 → +

Every 74-series chip needs power before it does anything. On this chip pin 14 (VCC) — the top-left pin — takes the positive supply.

Click Wire in the toolbar, click pin 14, then click a hole on the + rail above the chip. That's the whole move: wiring is always click one hole, click the other.

Both endpoints of your new wire show VCC — the wire touches the + rail, so its whole net is the 5 V supply.
intro.sim_basics.wire_vcc.hint.0.label: pin 14 (VCC)
intro.sim_basics.wire_vcc.hint.1.label: + rail
intro.sim_basics.wire_vcc.title: Connect Power (VCC)
intro.timer_555.astable.content:
Astable — No Stable State At All

Now let the chip trigger itself: tie trigger (pin 2) to the capacitor too. The cap voltage now works both comparators:

  1. Cap charges up through the resistors... hits ⅔ VCC → latch resets, OUT LOW, discharge opens.
  2. Cap drains down... hits ⅓ VCC → latch sets, OUT HIGH, charging resumes. Forever.

Sound familiar? It's the same shuttle-between-two-thresholds idea as the Schmitt RC oscillator in Lesson 5 — just with the thresholds provided by comparators instead of a special gate input. R and C set the frequency; the ratio of the two timing resistors sets how long HIGH lasts versus LOW.
intro.timer_555.astable.title: Astable: Free-Running
intro.timer_555.board_555.content:
Try It: One-Shot and Blinker

Two 555s, two personalities:

  • LEFT — monostable. Press the push button below the chip: the red LED fires one fixed-width pulse (about a tenth of a second — 1.1 × 10 k × 10 µF). Hold the button down; the pulse is the same length anyway.
  • RIGHT — astable. Nobody is touching it, and the green LED blinks away at roughly 1 Hz regardless. Its timing pair is 10 k with a 100 nF cap.

Both timing networks are ordinary resistors and capacitors sitting right on the board — no magic inside the chips beyond the comparators and latch you saw on page 1.

Complete this step by pressing the LEFT button to fire the one-shot.
intro.timer_555.board_555.hint.0.label: Press — fires the one-shot
intro.timer_555.board_555.hint.1.label: red: one fixed-width pulse
intro.timer_555.board_555.hint.2.label: green: astable, blinks on its own
intro.timer_555.board_555.title: Both on the Board
intro.timer_555.description: The most popular chip ever: comparators + an SR latch make precise pulses. One-shot (monostable) and free-running (astable) on one board.
intro.timer_555.inside_555.content:
The Most Popular Chip Ever Made

The 555 timer has been in production since 1972, with billions sold — and by now you already know every part inside it:

  • Three equal resistors divide VCC into ⅓ and ⅔ reference points (that string is where the name "555" comes from — 5 k + 5 k + 5 k).
  • Two comparators watch external pins against those references — two thresholds, exactly the trick from Lesson 5.
  • They SET and RESET a latch — the circuit from Lesson 4 — which drives the output pin.
  • A discharge transistor (pin 7) gives external capacitors a path to empty through.

Trigger (pin 2) dropping below ⅓ VCC sets the latch → OUT goes HIGH. Threshold (pin 6) rising above ⅔ VCC resets it → OUT goes LOW. Everything the 555 does comes from wiring resistors and capacitors around those two rules.
intro.timer_555.inside_555.title: Inside the 555
intro.timer_555.monostable.content:
Monostable — One Trigger, One Pulse

Wire a resistor from VCC to a capacitor, connect that node to threshold (pin 6) and discharge (pin 7), and use a button to pull trigger (pin 2) LOW:

  1. At rest, the latch is reset: OUT is LOW and the discharge transistor holds the cap empty.
  2. Press the button — trigger drops below ⅓ VCC, the latch sets: OUT snaps HIGH and the cap is released to charge through R.
  3. When the cap reaches ⅔ VCC, the threshold comparator resets the latch: OUT drops, the cap is dumped, ready for the next shot.

The pulse width is fixed by physics, not by how long you hold the button: T = 1.1 · R · C. Tap it or lean on it — same pulse. That's what makes the one-shot useful: it turns sloppy human-length events into precise machine-length ones.
intro.timer_555.monostable.title: Monostable: the One-Shot
intro.timer_555.quiz_mono_vs_astable.con:
Quiz 2 of 2

What's the key difference between monostable and astable operation?
intro.timer_555.quiz_trigger.content:
Quiz 1 of 2
